title: Emergency Guide: What to Do If Your Dog Ate a Box of Raisins
meta_desc: If your dog ate raisins, it is a medical emergency. Learn why raisins are toxic and why you must contact a veterinarian immediately for treatment.
content_html:
TL;DR: Raisin ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that can cause sudden kidney failure, requiring immediate veterinary intervention regardless of the amount eaten.

What is raisin toxicity and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Raisins are dried grapes, and both are known to be highly toxic to dogs. While the exact substance causing the reaction is still being researched, ingestion can lead to acute kidney failure. Unlike many other toxins, raisin toxicity is not always dose-dependent, meaning even a small amount can be fatal to some dogs regardless of their size or weight.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog eats raisins?

  • Contact your veterinarian or the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately, as this is a high-priority emergency.
  • Early intervention, such as inducing vomiting and starting fluid therapy, is critical to survival.
  • Immediate treatment is necessary to prevent permanent kidney damage or death.

How does taking a photo of the raisin packaging help my veterinarian?

  • Take a photo of the raisin box or packaging to help the vet confirm the exact product.
  • A photo allows the team to check for other toxic ingredients like xylitol or chocolate.
  • Packaging details help the medical team estimate the maximum volume your dog may have ingested.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of raisins, prompt decontamination is recommended if a significant amount was consumed. For recent ingestions of more than 1-2 g/kg in asymptomatic dogs, emesis should be induced with 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, not exceeding 45 mL), followed by activated charcoal, especially in large ingestions. If vomiting and/or diarrhea spontaneously develops within 6-12 hours of ingestion, or in cases of large ingestions, aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended. Monitor renal function and fluid balance during fluid therapy. Affected dogs may develop oliguric or anuric renal failure within 24-72 hours of exposure. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared with serum urea nitrogen levels. Other clinical signs include lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Consider other causes of renal failure (e.g., ethylene glycol, cholecalciferol) in the differential diagnosis.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What are the early signs of raisin poisoning?
faqs.answer: Early signs often include vomiting within 6 to 12 hours, followed by lethargy, loss of appetite, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog gets sick?
faqs.answer: No. By the time a dog shows clinical signs of raisin poisoning, significant kidney damage may have already occurred. Immediate decontamination is necessary.

faqs.question: Are all dogs affected by raisins?
faqs.answer: While some dogs may appear to have an individual tolerance, there is no way to predict which dogs will develop kidney failure. All ingestions must be treated as toxic.
